nodes.languageNotes: Guards on QET_COMMON_COLLECTION_PATH_RELATIVE_TO_BINARY_PATH_VAR and QET_LANG_PATH_RELATIVE_TO_BINARY_PATH_VAR are the macOS Debug build fix committed in this fork.

nodes.name: developer_options.cmake
nodes.summary: CMake module enabling developer-oriented build options: Qt deprecated-API warnings, message log context, BUILD_WITH_KF6 option (default ON), and QMFILES_AS_RESOURCE option (default ON) for embedding translations.

nodes.name: fetch_kdeaddons.cmake
nodes.summary: CMake module that either fetches KF6 KCoreAddons and KWidgetsAddons via FetchContent (BUILD_KF6=ON) at a pinned git tag, or finds them as system packages, then exposes KF6_PRIVATE_LIBRARIES for linking.
nodes.languageNotes: ECM_SKIP_QDOC_GENERATION=ON prevents duplicate-target CMake errors on systems with ECMGenerateQDoc installed (Homebrew extra-cmake-modules).

nodes.name: fetch_pugixml.cmake
nodes.summary: CMake module that either fetches the pugixml v1.15 XML library via FetchContent (BUILD_PUGIXML=ON) or locates the system-installed package.

nodes.name: fetch_singleapplication.cmake
nodes.summary: Fetches the SingleApplication library (v3.5.4) via CMake FetchContent from GitHub, configuring it to use QApplication as the base class.

nodes.name: git_last_commit_sha.cmake
nodes.summary: Retrieves the current HEAD git commit SHA at configure time and exposes it as the GIT_COMMIT_SHA preprocessor definition in compiled C++ source.

nodes.name: git_update_submodules.cmake
nodes.summary: Runs git submodule update --init --recursive at configure time to ensure all submodules are checked out before the build proceeds.

nodes.name: paths_compilation_installation.cmake
nodes.summary: Defines platform-specific installation and compilation paths (Linux, macOS, Windows) for QET binaries, elements, titleblocks, language files, and other resources, with a special Debug mode on macOS that uses binary-relative paths for developer workflow.
nodes.languageNotes: The macOS Debug branch sets QET_COMMON_COLLECTION_PATH_RELATIVE_TO_BINARY_PATH_VAR so that cmake/define_definitions.cmake can suppress the /usr/local/ prefix, enabling running from the build directory without installation.

nodes.name: qet_compilation_vars.cmake
nodes.summary: The central CMake build manifest for QElectroTech, enumerating all Qt components, private link libraries, UI form files, and every C++ source and header file that forms the QET executable.
nodes.languageNotes: With 758 lines, this file is the canonical list of all compiled translation units; adding or removing source files from the build requires editing this file.

nodes.name: start_options.cmake
nodes.summary: Activates optional command-line override flags (--common-elements-dir, --common-tbt-dir, --config-dir, --data-dir) by emitting corresponding preprocessor definitions that gate the runtime argument parsing.

layers.name: Application Shell
layers.description: Top-level Qt application singleton (QETApp), main window (QETDiagramEditor), diagram view, project view, and application entry point that wire together the entire QElectroTech session.

layers.name: Diagram Core
layers.description: Central domain model for QET projects and diagrams: QGraphicsScene-based Diagram, QetProject, BorderTitleBlock, diagram events, and their XML serialization and undo commands.

layers.name: Graphics Items
layers.description: All QGraphicsItem subclasses rendered on the diagram canvas — conductors, elements, cross-reference items, text items, image items, and table/view items.

layers.name: Element Editor
layers.description: Standalone graphical editor for authoring QET element symbols: the QGraphicsScene, interactive scene-event handlers, graphics primitives (arc, line, polygon, ellipse, text), and editor UI widgets.

layers.name: Domain Features
layers.description: Vertical feature modules implementing IEC 60617 domain concepts: terminal strip management, SQLite project database, conductor auto-numbering, search/replace, wiring-list export, and the elements collection browser.

layers.name: UI Forms & Dialogs
layers.description: Qt dialog and widget classes for conductor properties, title-block editing, element text, diagram export, alignment, and all other modal and dockable UI forms used during diagram editing.

layers.name: Shared Utilities
layers.description: Cross-cutting support code reused across features: XML helpers, property data classes, undo-command base infrastructure, conductor geometry (profile/segment), factory classes, icon registry, and Qt widget utilities.

layers.name: Project Support
layers.description: All non-C++ project infrastructure: GitHub Actions CI pipelines, CMake build modules, Flatpak/AppImage/Debian packaging scripts, translation files, icons, example schematics, man pages, and documentation.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: README.md is the best starting point: it describes QElectroTech as a libre CAD/CAE schematic editor for electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and process industries, targeting IEC 60617 wiring diagrams — not simulation and not PCB. Reading it first establishes the vocabulary (folio, element, conductor, terminal strip, title block) that every subsequent code step uses. The README also records supported platforms, build prerequisites, and the XML-based .qet file format that underpins the entire persistence layer.

tour.title: Application Entry Point
tour.description: main.cpp is where the Qt event loop starts. It installs a custom message handler for log rotation, then constructs a QETApp instance — the application singleton — and calls exec(). This file is intentionally thin: its only job is to hand control to QETApp and to collect the exit code. Tracing from here tells you exactly which objects are created at startup and in which order.
tour.languageLesson: Qt applications follow a pattern of QApplication subclass + exec(): the subclass handles all global state, while exec() runs the event loop. Keeping main.cpp minimal is a Qt idiom — all real initialisation belongs in the QApplication subclass constructor or in a dedicated startup method.

tour.title: Application Singleton: QETApp
tour.description: QETApp (qetapp.h / qetapp.cpp) is the central nervous system of QElectroTech. It is a QApplication subclass that owns every global resource: the element and title-block collection paths, the icon registry, the language translation, the application-wide SQLite project database, and the set of all open QETProject objects. With a fan-in of 80 — second highest in the entire graph — almost every other subsystem calls back into QETApp for shared services, making it the most important class to understand early.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's QApplication::instance() pattern provides a process-wide singleton without a hand-rolled Singleton class. QETApp adds static helper methods (QETApp::diagramEditor(), QETApp::project() etc.) so call sites throughout the codebase can reach global state without storing pointers.

tour.title: Global Namespace and Enums
tour.description: qet.h declares the QET C++ namespace, which acts as the project's shared vocabulary: enums for orientation, conductor end types, diagram areas, and export modes; type aliases; and small geometry utilities. qet.cpp implements the companion utility functions — direction helpers, bounding-box projections, and XML convenience wrappers. Because these types appear in virtually every other header, understanding qet.h gives you the common language spoken across all layers of the codebase.

tour.title: Project Model: QETProject
tour.description: QETProject (qetproject.h / qetproject.cpp) is the domain object that represents a single .qet file on disk. It owns the ordered list of Diagram objects (folios), the embedded element and title-block libraries, and the project-level auto-numbering configuration. It is also responsible for reading and writing the outer XML envelope of the project file. With a fan-in of 45, it is the third most-depended-upon header in the codebase, referenced by the main window, diagram editor, and every feature that needs cross-folio data.

tour.title: Diagram: The Core Scene Model
tour.description: Diagram (diagram.h / diagram.cpp) is the QGraphicsScene subclass that models a single folio. It owns all elements, conductors, images, and text objects placed on the drawing canvas, and it is responsible for their XML serialisation and deserialisation. With the highest fan-in in the entire graph (87), diagram.h is included by more files than any other header — every view, every undo command, every domain feature that touches a folio goes through this class. Understanding its API is the single most leveraged investment in the codebase.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's Model/View pattern: QGraphicsScene is the model (data + logic), QGraphicsView is the view (rendering + input). Keeping all business logic in Diagram rather than DiagramView means the scene can be manipulated headlessly — useful for batch export and automated tests.

tour.title: Main Window and Diagram View
tour.description: QETDiagramEditor (qetdiagrameditor.h / qetdiagrameditor.cpp) is the application's top-level MDI window. It hosts the menu bar, toolbars, docks, and the MDI workspace that holds multiple DiagramView widgets. DiagramView (diagramview.cpp) is the QGraphicsView subclass that renders a Diagram and translates raw mouse and keyboard events into editing operations. QETMainWindow (qetmainwindow.h) provides the abstract QMainWindow base shared by both the diagram editor and the element editor, unifying their status bar and dock management.

tour.title: Graphics Items: Elements, Conductors, Terminals
tour.description: The qetgraphicsitem/ directory contains all QGraphicsItem subclasses that live on the diagram canvas. Element (element.h) is the primary component class — a symbol loaded from the element library, with one or more Terminal attachment points. Conductor (conductor.h) is the electrical wire that connects two Terminals, computing its own routing path automatically. Terminal (terminal.h) is the small hotspot on an element where conductors attach and electrical connectivity is established. Together these three classes model the entire electrical graph of a schematic.
tour.languageLesson: QGraphicsItem subclasses override boundingRect(), paint(), and shape() for rendering, and mousePressEvent()/mouseMoveEvent() for interaction. QET adds a custom UserType integer to each item so the scene can query item types efficiently with qgraphicsitem_cast<>().

tour.title: Dynamic Element Text Items
tour.description: DynamicElementTextItem (dynamicelementtextitem.h / .cpp) is the rich-text label class attached to elements. Unlike static captions, it supports multiple text-source modes: static text, element info fields (reference, label, user variables), and formula-driven composite strings. With a fan-in of 26 it is one of the most-referenced graphics item headers, because it surfaces in the properties dialogs, the export pipeline, and the auto-numbering system. Understanding it explains how the schematic's annotation layer works.

tour.title: Interactive Editing: Event Strategy Pattern
tour.description: DiagramEventInterface (diagramevent/diagrameventinterface.h) is the abstract strategy base class for all interactive editing modes in DiagramView. Each mode — adding an element, drawing a conductor, placing a text — is a separate concrete subclass that implements the interface's virtual mouse and keyboard hooks. Swapping the active strategy changes the editor's behaviour without altering DiagramView itself. This strategy pattern keeps the view lean and makes it straightforward to add new editing modes.
tour.languageLesson: The Strategy pattern (GoF) is a common Qt idiom for mode-driven editors. A QGraphicsView holds a pointer to the current strategy; each mouse event is forwarded to the strategy rather than handled inline. This avoids a large switch-on-mode inside the view and keeps each mode's logic self-contained and testable in isolation.

tour.title: Undo/Redo Infrastructure
tour.description: QET implements every user action as a QUndoCommand subclass, enabling full undo/redo. AddGraphicsObjectCommand (undocommand/addgraphicsobjectcommand.h) is a representative example: it records the item being added, its diagram, and calls redo()/undo() to apply or reverse the placement. The companion QPropertyUndoCommand (QPropertyUndoCommand/qpropertyundocommand.h) is a generic, animation-aware command for property-level changes — it is the most-imported undo header in the graph (fan-in 38). The developer reference doc dev_doc/ID_of_QUndoCommand.txt enumerates every command's numeric ID, which is essential for QUndoCommand::mergeWith() coalescing.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's QUndoStack and QUndoCommand implement the Command pattern. Each command stores enough state to call redo() and undo() without side effects. mergeWith() allows the stack to coalesce consecutive same-type commands (e.g. property edits while dragging) into a single undo step.

tour.title: Element Editor
tour.description: QETElementEditor (editor/ui/qetelementeditor.h / .cpp) is the standalone window for authoring QET element symbols — the graphical primitives (lines, circles, arcs, polygons, text, terminals) that make up an element's appearance. Its underlying scene is ElementScene (editor/elementscene.h / .cpp), a QGraphicsScene subclass with a fan-in of 30 that manages primitive placement, XML round-tripping, and clipboard operations. The element editor is architecturally separate from the diagram editor: it can be opened independently and saves to .elmt files that are then consumed by the element library.

tour.title: Element Library and Factory
tour.description: ElementFactory (factory/elementfactory.h) instantiates Element graphics items from .elmt XML definitions at diagram load time. ElementPictureFactory (factory/elementpicturefactory.h) is the companion singleton that caches rendered QPixmap and QPicture representations used in the element collection panel and drag previews. Together they decouple the rest of the codebase from the details of element loading, parsing, and rendering — all callers ask the factory for an element by location path and receive a ready-to-use object.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's resource system stores element SVG/XML data inside the application binary via qrc files. ElementFactory reads from this virtual filesystem using QFile paths prefixed with ':/'. This means the built-in element library ships embedded in the executable and works without a filesystem installation.

tour.title: Auto-Numbering and Title Blocks
tour.description: AutoNumberingDockWidget (autoNum/ui/autonumberingdockwidget.h) drives the IEC-compliant automatic reference and folio numbering system, one of QET's signature domain features. It works alongside BorderTitleBlock (bordertitleblock.h), which manages the diagram's title block frame — the rectangular border with metadata fields (project name, drawing number, revision, author) that appears at the edge of every folio. The title block is templated and user-definable, and its data feeds directly into the auto-numbering pipeline.

tour.title: Terminal Strips
tour.description: TerminalStrip (TerminalStrip/terminalstrip.h) models a labelled terminal strip assembly — a real-world DIN-rail mounted row of connection terminals — and is one of QET's most sophisticated domain features. It collects Element instances whose type identifies them as terminal components, assigns them a strip identity, and generates the cross-reference tables and terminal strip diagrams required by IEC documentation standards. This is a vertical feature slice that touches elements, conductors, auto-numbering, and the project database.

tour.title: XML Serialisation and Project Database
tour.description: QETXML (qetxml.h) is the central serialisation namespace: it provides utility functions for converting Qt and QET types (colours, fonts, pen styles, enums) to and from XML attributes, and is included by 36 files — third highest fan-in among headers. The companion projectDataBase (dataBase/projectdatabase.h) wraps a per-project SQLite database that indexes elements and conductors for fast cross-reference queries. Together they form the persistence layer: QETXML handles the XML tree, while projectDataBase handles relational queries.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's QDomDocument / QDomElement API is a tree-structured XML interface. In Qt6, the QIODevice passed to QDomDocument::setContent() must be explicitly opened before the call — a stricter requirement than Qt5 that is a common Qt5-to-Qt6 porting trap.

tour.title: Build System and CI
tour.description: CMakeLists.txt (root) is the top-level build file that configures the Qt6 project, links Qt6, KF6, SQLite3, pugixml, and SingleApplication, and delegates to the cmake/ subdirectory modules. cmake/qet_compilation_vars.cmake is the real heart of the build: it enumerates all Qt components, every C++ source file, UI form, and resource file. The GitHub Actions pipeline in .github/workflows/windows-build.yml runs the Windows build on every push, while auto-doxygen.yml generates and publishes API reference documentation on each tag. Reading these files after the code steps reveals how the entire codebase is assembled and delivered.
tour.languageLesson: CMake's FetchContent module (used by cmake/fetch_singleapplication.cmake) downloads and builds dependencies at configure time, eliminating the need to vendor source or rely on system packages for third-party libraries like SingleApplication.
